title: Diffusion with conserved marginal distributions and information theory in fracton hydrodynamics
abstract: Diffusion with multipole-moment conservation gives rise to transport laws that generalize Fick's law and has attracted growing attention following experimental advances in strongly tilted optical lattices. It was recently shown that conserving complete multipole-moment groups leads to subdiffusive dynamics governed by a nonlinear diffusion equation, raising the question of whether hydrodynamic equations would also be nonlinear when the conservation law is imposed only at the subsystem level. Here we show that subsystem symmetries generically produce nonlinear hydrodynamic equations with shear-only transport, in which any localization present in the initial marginal distributions is preserved at long times by the conservation of those marginals. A linear regime emerges only as a limiting case for small fluctuations around a uniform background. We derive the deterministic and fluctuating parts of the hydrodynamic equations in arbitrary dimensions and obtain the corresponding maximum-entropy equilibrium distributions under constrained marginals. We also show that marginal-conserving diffusion provides a concrete hydrodynamic realization of partial multipole-moment conservation, and we offer an information-theoretic interpretation in which total correlation decays monotonically even when pairwise mutual information does not.
summary: This paper explores how conservation laws applied to subsystems (marginals) rather than the whole system lead to nonlinear hydrodynamic equations. The authors show that these symmetries result in shear-only transport and the preservation of initial spatial localization. They also demonstrate that while total correlation always decreases toward equilibrium, pairwise mutual information can temporarily increase in dimensions higher than two. This work connects the physics of fracton-like systems with the principles of information theory and entropy production.
main_problem: Investigating the nature of nonlinear hydrodynamic equations and information-theoretic evolution in systems with subsystem symmetries, specifically regarding the conservation of marginal distributions.
main_result: Subsystem symmetries generically produce nonlinear hydrodynamic equations with shear-only transport, where initial localization in marginals is preserved; additionally, total correlation decays monotonically while pairwise mutual information can exhibit non-monotonic behavior in dimensions d > 2.
method: The authors use a gradient expansion of a microscopic master equation to derive a continuum hydrodynamic limit, apply the maximum-entropy principle to find equilibrium distributions, and utilize information-theoretic functionals (KL divergence, total correlation) to analyze dynamics.
model_or_system: A lattice-based particle system (e.g., 2D square lattice) featuring shear-only transport and subsystem symmetries where mass density is conserved along hyperplanes, providing a hydrodynamic realization of partial multipole-moment conservation.
key_observables: Particle density, current tensor, marginal distributions, Shannon entropy, KL divergence, Total Correlation, and pairwise Mutual Information.
important_parameters: Dynamical scaling exponent z = 2k, diffusion constant D, and dimensionality d.
main_assumptions: Assumes vanishing boundary terms during integration by parts and that noise is Gaussian, local in space and time.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the microscopic shear model on a lattice; Figure 2 shows numerical simulations of density evolution from a mixture of Gaussians to a product-form equilibrium; Figure 4 shows the monotonic decrease of KL divergence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of subsystem symmetries, derives the nonlinear hydrodynamic equations via continuum limit, generalizes the theory to higher dimensions, explores the connection to partial multipole-moment conservation, and concludes with an information-theoretic analysis of entropy and correlation dynamics.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental link between fracton hydrodynamics and information theory, offering concrete observables like non-monotonic mutual information that can be tested in site-resolved quantum gas microscopes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Microscopic model for shear-only transport, where one-dimensional marginal distributions are conserved, and the subsystem symmetries include conservation of mass along ev- ery row and column of the lattice. Purple lattice sites are occupied.

FIG. 2. Numerical results for two-dimensional marginal-conserving diffusion. (A) The initial distribution at t = 0, (B) an intermediate distribution from numerics at t = 2 × 105, (C) the final distribution from numerics at t = 2 × 106, (D) the final distribution from theory (i.e. the product distribution). (E) Marginal distributions along each axis for initial, final numerics, and final theory. (F) Agreement (Pearson r = 0.999) between ρeq(x, y) equilibrium distributions from theory and numerics, where each data point is the density at some point (x, y).

FIG. 3. Generalized microscopic model for (A) d = 3, k = 3, where two-dimensional (and one-dimensional) marginal distribu- tions are conserved, and the associated subsystem symmetries include conservation of mass along every line and every plane; and for (B) d = 3, k = 2, where only one-dimensional marginal distributions are conserved, and the associated subsystem symmetries include conservation of mass along every plane but not every line. Purple lattice sites are occupied.
FIG. 4. KL divergence DKL[ρ||ρeq] for two-dimensional marginal-conserving diffusion plotted as a function of time, showing monotonic decrease towards zero.
